skill.name: learn
skill.description: Discover, install, update, scan, and rate AI agent skills from agentskill.sh during an active agent session.
skill.summary: Find, vet, install, update, and rate agent skills from agentskill.sh during a session.
skill.author: agentskill-sh

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many critical and high patterns, but most are false positives from README examples and the security pattern library. Confirmed concerns are intended but high impact: the skill fetches remote skill content, writes into active agent skill directories, can self-update, and sends feedback or security metadata to agentskill.sh. No confirmed malicious intent was found, but this is not safe to publish without stronger trust, consent, and update controls.

high_findings.title: Remote Skill Installation Writes Active Agent Instructions
high_findings.description: The skill fetches remote skill content from agentskill.sh, scans it, and writes it into detected agent skill directories. This is intended package-manager behavior, but it creates a high impact supply chain path because installed skill instructions can later influence agent tool use.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The install flow directly combines remote fetch, security gating, and local writes into active skill paths. The behavior is explicit and intended, but remote skill content can become executable agent instructions.

high_findings.title: Self-Update Can Overwrite the Installer With Lower Security Threshold
high_findings.description: Before subcommands, the skill checks for a newer version, fetches it, scans it, and overwrites the current skill if the score is at least 50. This threshold is weaker than the install flow block threshold of 70 and increases compromise impact if the remote service or update content is compromised.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The self-update steps and the numeric threshold are directly documented. The risk is supply chain compromise rather than proven malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: External Data Can Become Second-Order Prompt Injection
medium_findings.description: The skill relies on WebFetch responses for search, installation, version checks, reports, and feedback. The security guidance itself notes that external data sources and fetched skill content can contain instructions that alter agent behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly fetches external content and its own documentation identifies this as a prompt injection class. I did not find evidence that fetched text bypasses all review, so the finding is medium rather than critical.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Feedback Sends Usage Metadata by Default
medium_findings.description: The skill can auto-rate used skills and submit score, comment, platform, timestamp, agent name, and auto-rating metadata. It says no PII is sent, but comments about what worked or failed can still disclose private workflow details.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The submitted fields and opt-out behavior are documented. The privacy risk depends on generated comment content, so confidence is high for data transmission and moderate for sensitivity.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Matched Security Reference Examples
low_findings.description: Many critical patterns, including credential paths, destructive commands, jailbreak keywords, pickle, and pipe-to-shell examples, appear inside the security pattern library as examples of what to detect. These are false positives in semantic context and not instructions to execute those payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious strings are inside tables and scanner guidance with headings that define detection patterns. No evidence was found that these examples are executed by the skill.

low_findings.title: README Install Paths Trigger Hidden Directory Findings
low_findings.description: README examples reference hidden agent skill directories such as Claude, Cursor, Codex, Windsurf, and Gemini locations. These are installation documentation paths, not credential theft or stealth file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are shown in install and platform support documentation. They point to agent skill directories rather than secrets, and no exfiltration action is attached.

dangerous_patterns.title: Fetch Then Install Remote Skill Content
dangerous_patterns.description: The install flow fetches remote skill content and writes it into the local agent skill directory after security scoring.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The fetch and write steps are directly documented in the install flow. The danger comes from remote content becoming persistent agent instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Self-Update of Current Skill
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill checks its remote version, fetches a new copy if the hash differs, and overwrites itself when the scan score passes a documented threshold.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The self-update behavior is explicit. I did not find malicious intent, but the pattern is sensitive for supply chain security.

dangerous_patterns.title: Optional External Scanner Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The local scan workflow asks the agent to run external security scanners such as mcp-scan, trufflehog, gitleaks, and detect-secrets when available.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are legitimate security tooling and are not hidden. They still require external command execution and can inspect local files selected for scanning.

content.value_statement: Finding the right agent skill during a task can interrupt work and require manual browsing. This skill searches agentskill.sh, shows security context, and installs selected skills inside the current agent environment.
actual_capabilities: Searches agentskill.sh by query, direct owner and slug, URL, or trending list.
Shows matching skills with author, install count, and security score before installation.
Fetches skill content, runs a security review flow, and blocks low scoring skills.
Lists, updates, removes, and installs skill files in detected agent skill directories.
Can recommend skills from project context such as dependencies, config files, and branch name.
Submits install, feedback, and security report metadata to agentskill.sh endpoints.
limitations: It depends on agentskill.sh availability and the integrity of its API responses.
Remote skill content can change, so high impact installs still require human review.
It needs write access to the detected skill directory to install or update skills.
Pattern-based scanning can miss semantic prompt injection or supply chain attacks.

use_cases.title: Find a missing capability during a task
use_cases.description: Search for a relevant agent skill and install it without leaving the current session.

use_cases.title: Audit a local skill before use
use_cases.description: Scan a local SKILL.md file and review network, filesystem, command, and prompt injection risks.

use_cases.title: Maintain a shared agent setup
use_cases.description: List installed skills, check for updates, remove unused skills, and collect usage feedback.

prompt_templates.title: Find Skills
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner search for a useful capability.
prompt_templates.prompt: Find agent skills for React accessibility work. Show the best options and their security scores before installing anything.

prompt_templates.title: Install Known Skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Install a known marketplace skill with review first.
prompt_templates.prompt: Install the skill @owner/slug only after showing its author, install count, security score, and a short summary.

prompt_templates.title: Scan Local Skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Review a local skill before adding it to an agent environment.
prompt_templates.prompt: Scan the SKILL.md in this directory and explain any security issues, risky endpoints, file access, or command patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Manage Installed Skills
prompt_templates.prompt: List installed skills, check for updates, and update only skills that pass the security scan. Ask before writing files.

output_examples.input: Find a skill for writing product launch emails.
output_examples.output: A ranked table of matching skills with author, install count, security score, short descriptions, and an install choice prompt.

output_examples.input: Scan this local skill before I use it.
output_examples.output: A security report with an overall status, score, issue table, network endpoints, file access, and shell command summary.
A list of available updates, a confirmation prompt, and a post-update summary for each skill that passed review.

best_practices: Review the security score and issue list before installing any remote skill.
Disable auto-rating when comments could reveal project details or private workflow information.
Use exact owner and slug names for trusted skills instead of installing from broad search results.
anti_patterns: Do not install skills with low security scores just to continue a task faster.
Do not treat fetched skill content as trusted system instructions without review.
Do not run updates in sensitive projects without checking what will be overwritten.

faq.question: Does this skill install other skills automatically?
faq.answer: It presents options and uses confirmation flows before normal installs, but update and rating behavior should still be reviewed.

faq.question: What external service does it use?
faq.answer: It uses agentskill.sh API endpoints for search, install content, version checks, install tracking, feedback, and security reports.

faq.question: Can it write files?
faq.answer: Yes. It writes installed skills into detected agent skill directories and can remove or overwrite skill files during management tasks.

faq.question: Does it execute downloaded code?
faq.answer: No direct execution was found, but installed skill instructions can later influence agent behavior and tool use.

faq.question: Why is the security risk high?
faq.answer: It fetches remote instructions, installs them into active agent environments, and includes a self-update path for the installer itself.

faq.question: What should users check before publishing or using it?
faq.answer: Users should require stronger update thresholds, clearer consent for telemetry, and manual review for remote skill content.
